All true Christians are brothers and sisters to one another. Faithfulness runs through every aspect and relationship of the Christian life. Faith, hope, and love are the three main virtues in the Christian life, and appropriate topics for prayer and thanksgiving. The more we fix our hopes on the reward in the next world, the freer we will be in doing good with our earthly possessions. These were treasured up for them, and no enemy could deprive them of it. The gospel is the word of truth, and we may safely rely on it. And all who hear the gospel should produce its fruit, obey it, and have their principles and lives formed according to it. Worldly love arises either from considerations of self-interest or from similarity in manners; carnal love comes from a desire for pleasure. Something corrupt, selfish, and base always clings to these kinds of love. But Christian love arises from the Holy Spirit, and is full of holiness.

The apostle consistently prayed that believers might be filled with knowledge of God's will, in all wisdom. Good words are not enough without good works. He who undertakes to give strength to his people is a powerful God, and of glorious power. The blessed Spirit is the source of this. When we pray for spiritual strength, we should not feel limited or confined by promises, nor should we be so in our hopes and desires. The grace of God in believers’ hearts is God's power; and there is glory in this power. The specific purpose of this strength was to endure suffering. There is work to be done even when we are suffering. Amidst all their trials they gave thanks to the Father of our Lord Jesus, whose special grace fitted them to share in the inheritance provided for the saints. To bring about this change, those were made willing subjects of Christ who had been slaves of Satan. All who are destined for heaven eventually will be prepared for heaven now. Those who have the inheritance of sons also have the education and disposition of sons. By faith in Christ they enjoyed this redemption, as the purchase of his atoning blood, by which forgiveness of sins and all other spiritual blessings were bestowed. Surely then we shall consider it a blessing to be delivered from Satan’s kingdom and brought into that of Christ, knowing that all trials will soon end, and that every believer will be found among those who come out of great tribulation.

Christ in his human nature is the visible revelation of the invisible God, and whoever has seen Him has seen the Father. Let us adore these mysteries with humble faith, and behold the glory of the Lord in Christ Jesus. He was born or begotten before all creation, before any creature was made; this is the way Scripture represents eternity, and by which the eternity of God is represented to us. Since all things were created by Him, they were created for Him; being made by his power, they were made according to his pleasure, and for his praise and glory. He not only created them all at first, but it is by the word of his power that they are sustained. Christ as Mediator is the Head of the body, the church; all grace and strength come from him; and the church is his body. All fullness dwells in Him: a fullness of merit and righteousness, of strength and grace for us. God showed his justice in requiring full satisfaction. This method of redeeming mankind by the death of Christ was most suitable. Here is presented to our view the way of being reconciled. And even though God hates sin, it pleased God to reconcile fallen man to himself. If we are convinced that we were enemies in our minds through wicked works, and that we are now reconciled to God by the sacrifice and death of Christ in our nature, we shall not try to explain away or fully comprehend these mysteries; but we will see the glory of this plan of redemption and rejoice in the hope set before us. If this is so, that God’s love for us is so great, what should we do for God now? Be frequent in prayer, abound in holy duties, and live not to yourselves, but to Christ. Christ died for us. But why? So that we would still live in sin? No; but so that we might die to sin, and live henceforth not to ourselves, but to Him.

Both the sufferings of the Head and of the members are called the sufferings of Christ, and they make up one body of suffering. But He suffered for the redemption of the church; we suffer for other reasons; for we only slightly taste that cup of affliction which Christ first drank deeply. A Christian may be said to fill up what remains of the sufferings of Christ when he takes up his cross and, following Christ’s example, patiently bears the afflictions God assigns to him. Let us be thankful that God has revealed to us mysteries hidden from ages and generations, and has shown the riches of his glory among us. As Christ is preached among us, let us seriously inquire whether He dwells and reigns in us; for this alone can guarantee our assured hope of His glory. We must remain faithful to death, through all trials, so that we may receive the crown of life, and obtain the end of our faith, the salvation of our souls.
